title: Parallel Processing (Batching)

content.question: What are you doing scattered that you could batch into one block?
sop: List your recurring task types, emails, content, calls, admin, creative work
Group each type into a single dedicated block on your calendar
Count how many context switches your current schedule forces per day (see calculation, page 5)
Turn off notifications for every task type except the one you're actively batching
Protect the batch block as non-negotiable for at least 2 weeks before judging results
content.explainer: Task switching doesn't just cost the seconds it takes to open a new tab, it costs the minutes it takes your attention to fully re-enter the task you left, a cost that's invisible because it doesn't show up as a line item anywhere. Batching removes the switch entirely instead of trying to do it faster.
questions: What are you doing scattered that you could batch into one block?
How many times today did you switch between two completely different types of task, and how long did it take you to get back into the first one?
What's the one task type you keep doing in tiny scattered pieces instead of one committed block?
content.equation_example: Switches: 15 task-type changes in a day (count). Efficiency is proportional to 1 divided by 15, approximately 0.067, a low efficiency index. Cut switches to 3 and efficiency is proportional to 1 divided by 3, approximately 0.33, five times higher. Fewer switches (a lower count) means a higher efficiency index; more switching means more of your day burned on refocusing instead of producing.
content.closing_statement: Every switch has a hidden refocusing cost you're paying.

caption:
Principle Nº 214: Parallel Processing (Batching)

Group similar tasks. Task switching doesn't just cost the seconds it takes to open a new tab, it costs the minutes it takes your attention to fully re-enter the task you left, a cost that's invisible because it doesn't show up as a line item anywhere.

Ask yourself: What are you doing scattered that you could batch into one block?
